comment_text.sn7.4:1.2: Bilaṅga (here, “bitter”) is allowed with other medicinal fruits at Kd 6:6.1.4. It is Sanskrit viḍaṅga, identified as embelia ribes or “false black pepper”. It is normally given as an example of poor food (SN 3.19:2.5).
comment_text.sn7.4:1.3: The commentary says he was too angry to speak because his brothers, in the previous three discourses, had ordained.

root_text.sn7.4:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā rājagahe viharati veḷuvane kalandakanivāpe.
root_text.sn7.4:1.3: “bhāradvājagotto kira brāhmaṇo samaṇassa gotamassa santike agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajito”ti kupito anattamano yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā tuṇhībhūto ekamantaṁ aṭṭhāsi.
root_text.sn7.4:1.4: Atha kho bhagavā bilaṅgikassa bhāradvājassa brāhmaṇassa cetasā cetoparivitakkamaññāya bilaṅgikaṁ bhāradvājaṁ brāhmaṇaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn7.4:3.1: Evaṁ vutte, bilaṅgikabhāradvājo brāhmaṇo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn7.4:3.4: Aññataro ca panāyasmā bhāradvājo arahataṁ ahosīti.

translation_text.sn7.4:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.
translation_text.sn7.4:1.2: The brahmin Bhāradvāja the Bitter heard a rumor that
translation_text.sn7.4:1.3: a brahmin of the Bhāradvāja clan had gone forth from the lay life to homelessness in the presence of the ascetic Gotama. Angry and displeased he went to the Buddha and stood silently to one side.
translation_text.sn7.4:1.4: Then the Buddha, knowing Bhāradvāja the Bitter’s train of thought, addressed him in verse:
translation_text.sn7.4:2.1: “Whoever wrongs a man who has done no wrong,
translation_text.sn7.4:2.2: a pure man who has not a blemish,
translation_text.sn7.4:2.3: the evil backfires on the fool,
translation_text.sn7.4:3.1: When he said this, the brahmin Bhāradvāja the Bitter said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn7.4:3.4: And Venerable Bhāradvāja became one of the perfected.
